serving.natural_language_portion: One medium takeaway cup of flavoured bubble tea with milk, approximately 500 ml (500 g), as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured bubble tea with milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured bubble tea with milk (cr_id D08010027). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened milk tea beverages — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and tapioca-based beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for flavoured bubble tea with milk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 (high) is estimated based on analogous entries for sweetened milk tea (GI ~65–72) and the contribution of tapioca pearls (GI ~56–81 depending on preparation). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 80.05) / 100 = 56.0 for a 500 g serving. This is an analogous estimate; direct testing of this specific product formulation has not been performed.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; potential trans fat and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit flavoured bubble tea with milk, as the high sugar load (13.11 g/100 g, 65.55 g per 500 ml serving) independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidelines, and non-dairy creamers commonly used in bubble tea may contribute saturated and trans fats that further increase cardiovascular risk; request 0% sugar and no toppings if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sugar-sweetened beverage (GI ~70) with 13.11 g sugar/100 g and 16.01 g carbohydrate/100 g; tapioca pearls add additional high-GI refined starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid flavoured bubble tea with milk, as it is a high-GI (GI ~70) sugar-sweetened beverage delivering approximately 65.5 g sugar and 80 g carbohydrate per 500 ml serving, causing a rapid and significant postprandial glycaemic spike; if consumed, request 0% sugar, no tapioca pearls, and limit portion to no more than 100–150 ml per occasion with close blood glucose monitoring per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar load (13.11 g/100 g) promoting dyslipidaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis; likely saturated and trans fat from non-dairy creamer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid flavoured bubble tea with milk, as the high free sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) promotes triglyceride elevation and hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and the non-dairy creamer component typically contains palm oil and potentially partially hydrogenated oils with trans fats, all of which are independently associated with increased LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines; likely trans fat from non-dairy creamer
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid flavoured bubble tea with milk, as the high sugar load (13.11 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine production, and non-dairy creamers in bubble tea typically contain partially hydrogenated oils with trans fats that are strongly pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured bubble tea with milk is a popular sweetened cold beverage widely sold at takeaway tea shops across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by combining brewed tea (typically black tea or a flavoured tea base) with milk or non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and a choice of flavouring such as taro, matcha, strawberry, or brown sugar. The drink is typically served over ice in a sealed plastic cup with a wide straw. Optional toppings include tapioca pearls (boba), which are chewy, starchy spheres made from tapioca starch and cooked in sugar syrup. The beverage has a sweet, creamy, mildly tea-flavoured taste with a smooth texture; tapioca pearls add a distinctive chewy mouthfeel. Per 100 g, it provides 85 kcal, 0.34 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 16.01 g carbohydrate, and 13.11 g sugar, with very low dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (13 mg). The high sugar content (13.11 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). A standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 425 kcal, 80 g carbohydrate, and 65.5 g sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), with a glycemic load of approximately 56 per 500 ml serving, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. This beverage is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high free sugar content, high glycemic load, and the likely presence of saturated and trans fats from non-dairy creamer. It is a culturally significant beverage in Singapore's food landscape, commonly consumed as a snack or afternoon refreshment, and is available with customisable sugar levels (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) and ice levels.

Can I drink bubble tea if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010027) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary regional approved source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which reduces GI-specific confidence to medium, but overall record confidence remains high given the exact nutrient source match.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific flavoured bubble tea with milk formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D08010027) as of 2026. The HPB source provides only the core macronutrient and selected mineral values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 80.05 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 56.0. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and tapioca-based beverages; no direct GI measurement for this specific product formulation is available in the approved GI source database. Non-dairy creamer is commonly used in Singaporean bubble tea preparations and typically contains palm oil; trans fat content is not confirmed in the HPB source but is noted as a clinical caution. Dietary fibre of 0.03 g/100 g is effectively negligible. Calcium value of 3.0 mg/100 g is notably low, suggesting the milk component may be primarily non-dairy creamer rather than fresh milk.
